YOLO (You Only Look Once) for Real-Time Object Detection¶

YOLO (You Only Look Once) is a deep learning–based object detection algorithm designed for fast and real-time image processing. Unlike traditional object detection methods that analyze different regions of an image separately, YOLO processes the entire image in a single pass through a neural network, making it extremely efficient.

In YOLO, the input image is divided into a grid of cells (for example, S × S). Each grid cell is responsible for detecting objects whose center lies inside that cell. For every grid cell, the network predicts:

  • Bounding box coordinates (position and size of the object)
  • Confidence score indicating the probability that an object exists in the predicted box
  • Class probabilities representing the category of the detected object

By combining these predictions, YOLO can detect multiple objects and classify them simultaneously in a single forward pass of the network.

Because the entire image is processed at once, YOLO captures global contextual information, which helps reduce false detections compared to methods that examine small regions independently.

Due to its high speed and efficiency, YOLO is widely used in applications such as:

  • Autonomous driving
  • Video surveillance
  • Robotics
  • Real-time video analysis
  • Smart cameras

Is YOLO Better than CNN?¶

YOLO is not necessarily "better than CNN" because YOLO itself is built using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). The two are not directly comparable. Instead:

  • CNN (Convolutional Neural Network) is a general deep learning architecture used for image-related tasks such as classification, detection, and segmentation.
  • YOLO (You Only Look Once) is a specific object detection algorithm that uses CNN as its backbone.

Key Differences¶

Feature CNN (Basic) YOLO
Purpose Mainly image classification Object detection and classification
Output Class label Bounding boxes + object classes
Speed Moderate Very fast (real-time detection)
Detection ability Cannot locate objects by default Detects multiple objects in one image

Example¶

  • A basic CNN might predict:
    "This image contains a dog."

  • YOLO can predict:
    "There is a dog at this location (bounding box) and a person at another location."

Why YOLO is Popular¶

YOLO is widely used because it can:

  • Detect multiple objects simultaneously
  • Process images in real time
  • Perform detection using a single forward pass of the CNN

Conclusion¶

  • CNN is the underlying neural network architecture.

  • YOLO is an object detection framework built on CNN that performs detection and classification in a single step.

  • Each bounding box predicts objects inside and gives confidence score. It gives position and label.

Repositary link: https://github.com/AlexeyAB/darknet

Downloading and cloning Darknet Repositary:¶

In [1]:
!git clone https://github.com/AlexeyAB/darknet

Cloning into 'darknet'...
remote: Enumerating objects: 15941, done.
remote: Counting objects: 100% (32/32), done.
remote: Compressing objects: 100% (24/24), done.
remote: Total 15941 (delta 14), reused 9 (delta 8), pack-reused 15909 (from 2)
Receiving objects: 100% (15941/15941), 14.51 MiB | 7.05 MiB/s, done.
Resolving deltas: 100% (10720/10720), done.
In [2]:
ls

darknet/  sample_data/
In [3]:
cd darknet/

/content/darknet
In [4]:
ls

3rdparty/               data/                   net_cam_v3.sh*
build/                  docker-compose.yml      net_cam_v4.sh*
CCM/                    Dockerfile.cpu          package.xml
cfg/                    Dockerfile.gpu          README.md
cmake/                  image_yolov3.sh*        scripts/
CMakeLists.txt          image_yolov4.sh*        src/
DarknetConfig.cmake.in  include/                vcpkg.json
darknet_images.py       json_mjpeg_streams.sh*  video_yolov3.sh*
darknet.py              LICENSE                 video_yolov4.sh*
darknet_video.py        Makefile

Compile the library¶

In [5]:
!make

mkdir -p ./obj/
mkdir -p backup
mkdir -p results
chmod +x *.sh
g++ -std=c++11 -std=c++11 -Iinclude/ -I3rdparty/stb/include -Wall -Wfatal-errors -Wno-unused-result -Wno-unknown-pragmas -fPIC -rdynamic -Ofast -c ./src/image_opencv.cpp -o obj/image_opencv.o

In [5]:

Download the pre trained weights¶

Not compiled with OpenCV, saving to predictions.jpg instead
In [9]:
import cv2
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

image = cv2.imread('predictions.jpg')
In [10]:
def show_detection(path):
  image = cv2.imread(path)
  plt.imshow(cv2.cvtColor(image, cv2.COLOR_BGR2RGB))
In [11]:
show_detection('predictions.jpg')

Darknet and GPU¶

In [12]:
import tensorflow as tf
tf.test.gpu_device_name()
Out[12]:
'/device:GPU:0'
In [14]:
ls

3rdparty/               data/                   obj/
backup/                 docker-compose.yml      package.xml
build/                  Dockerfile.cpu          predictions.jpg
CCM/                    Dockerfile.gpu          README.md
cfg/                    image_yolov3.sh*        results/
cmake/                  image_yolov4.sh*        scripts/
CMakeLists.txt          include/                src/
darknet*                json_mjpeg_streams.sh*  vcpkg.json
DarknetConfig.cmake.in  LICENSE                 video_yolov3.sh*
darknet_images.py       Makefile                video_yolov4.sh*
darknet.py              net_cam_v3.sh*          yolov4.weights
darknet_video.py        net_cam_v4.sh*
Line Purpose
OPENCV=1 Enables OpenCV for image/video processing
GPU=1 Enables GPU acceleration
CUDNN=1 Enables NVIDIA cuDNN for faster deep learning
In [15]:
# Enable OpenCV
!sed -i 's/OPENCV=0/OPENCV=1/' Makefile

# Enable GPU
!sed -i 's/GPU=0/GPU=1/' Makefile

# Enable cuDNN
!sed -i 's/CUDNN=0/CUDNN=1/' Makefile
In [18]:
#!make
In [19]:

In [23]:
show_detection('predictions.jpg')
In [29]:
def show_detection(path):
  image = cv2.imread(path)
  plt.imshow(cv2.cvtColor(image, cv2.COLOR_BGR2RGB))
  plt.savefig('/content/drive/MyDrive/MCV_Continuatin_inGoogle_Colab/13_YOLO/output.jpg')
In [31]:
show_detection('predictions.jpg')
